title: The interpreter crashes when specializing bound method calls on unbound objects
author: devdanzin
body:
# Crash report

### What happened?

The following code segfaults the interpreter:

```python
import glob

for x in range(3):
    str_globber = glob._StringGlobber(None, None)
str_globber.selector(set())

try:
    str_globber.selector([True, True, False])
except:
    pass

globber_base = glob._GlobberBase(0, 0, 0, 0)
globber_base.selector(list)
```

(Edited by @ZeroIntensity) Smaller reproducer:

```py
def call(part):
    part.pop()

call(['a'])
try:
    call(list)
except TypeError:
    raise
```

Backtrace:

270             return ob->ob_type;
(gdb) bt

Found using [fusil](https://github.com/devdanzin/fusil) by @vstinner.

### CPython versions tested on:

CPython main branch

### Operating systems tested on:

Linux

### Output from running 'python -VV' on the command line:

Python 3.14.0a6+ (heads/main:3a8cefba0b6, Apr  1 2025, 21:31:55) [GCC 11.4.0]

<!-- gh-linked-prs -->
### Linked PRs
* gh-132000
* gh-132262
<!-- /gh-linked-prs -->

comments.author: vstinner
comments.body:
> Looks like a 3.14 regression.

I can reproduce the crash on Python 3.13.

comments.author: ZeroIntensity
comments.body: Hmm, I couldn't. Let me double check.

comments.author: vstinner
comments.body: I can reproduce the crash on a Python 3.13 release build (PGO+LTO). I cannot reproduce the crash on a Python 3.13 debug build (`--with-pydebug CFLAGS="-O0"`).

comments.author: ZeroIntensity
comments.body: Ah, this *doesn't* occur on a 3.13.2 release build, so this will be a neat surprise in the upcoming release next week.

comments.author: vstinner
comments.body:
The crash on Python 3.13.2 using the Fedora 41 package:

```console
$ cat x.py 
def call(part):
    part.pop()
call(['a'])
try:
    call(list)
except TypeError:
    raise

$ python3.13 -VV
Python 3.13.2 (main, Feb  4 2025, 00:00:00) [GCC 14.2.1 20250110 (Red Hat 14.2.1-7)]

$ python3.13 x.py 
Erreur de segmentation (core dumped)
```

comments.author: ZeroIntensity
comments.body: I can reproduce using that script on 3.13.2, but not the original one that @devdanzin provided.
